Article Title: Meet Aashna Doshi, Indian-origin Google engineer who quit her dream job at 23 to build a podcast and AI startup
Article Text:
Why a Google Software Engineer Quit Her New York Job

At just 23, Aashna Doshi walked away from one of the most popular jobs in tech at Google to build a startup and pursue a creative idea that had been growing alongside her engineering career.Doshi is a former software engineer based in New York City. She made the decision earlier this year after joining Google and building a side project that eventually reshaped her career path. Her journey began even before she graduated from Georgia Tech, when she received a full-time offer from the tech giant in February 2024.At the time, the role was based in California, but Doshi had her sights set on New York. Faced with a competitive job market, she made a bold call.In a coversation with the Business Insider, she explained the turning point:“I took a big risk to get my Google job. Now I'm taking another one by leaving it.”She turned down the initial offer, a decision that soon paid off. Two months later, she secured a software engineering role at Google based in New York City, allowing her to move to the city she wanted.She enjoyed her time at Google and valued the learning experience, but Doshi said she gradually felt the need for something beyond her day-to-day technical work.“I really enjoyed my experience at Google. I was learning every day and meeting a lot of smart people from diverse backgrounds.”However, she said engineering work alone did not fully satisfy her creative interests. That led her to start a podcast in early 2025 called “0 to 1”, alongside a co-host who also worked in big tech.The podcast focused on conversations with founders, engineers, executives and creators, exploring personal journeys.“The name for the show, in addition to being a nod to our backgrounds in software engineering, came from the idea that there's so much value between zero — where someone started — and one — who they are today.”It began as a side project, but quickly gained traction. The podcast passed 100,000 YouTube views in its first year and helped the duo connect with senior leaders from companies such as Amazon and Microsoft. Many early guests came through cold messages and personal networks.Doshi said the platform became a tool for career growth and networking.Without the podcast, it was hard to imagine how we might go about connecting with someone high up in a big company.As the audience grew, so did the ambition behind it. Eventually, Doshi said the experience changed how she viewed her own career trajectory.In May, she made the decision to leave Google and focus full-time on building an AI startup called Bounty with her co-host. The company is designed as an outcome-based AI marketplace where businesses can assign tasks such as lead generation, outreach, or recruitment sourcing, and pay only when results are delivered.“It felt like the right time to leave Google.”She explained that working inside a large tech company gave her valuable experience, but also made her want more independence and faster execution.“At a Big Tech company, you're one piece of a very large machine, and I craved the ability to make decisions, move fast, and see the direct results of my work.”Doshi also said the current wave of AI tools influenced her decision. Leaving Google meant giving up financial stability, something she acknowledged was not easy. Her startup is still pre-revenue, and she is currently on a founder salary.“Leaving Google was a risk, but I've always believed that if you feel a strong enough pull toward something, you have to be willing to walk away from good in pursuit of something that could be great.”
Article Summary: Aashna Doshi is a former software engineer based in New York City. She left Google earlier this year after building a side project that reshaped her career path. She is now building an AI startup called Bounty with her co-host. The company is designed as an outcome-based AI marketplace.

Article Title: Anthropic's engineering leader Fiona Fung admits that coding with Claude Code 'hurt' developers; says: As engineers relied more on AI agents, they became ...
Article Text:
“Lenny's Podcast,”

“The thing that we found interesting on the

Claude

Code team is, after a while, we felt it could start being a lonely experience because we all started just working with our agents so much.”

How Anthropic is solving the ‘isolation problem’ of engineers using AI coding agents



"maker time."

“Everybody uses Claude Cowork. Everybody uses a flow so differently. When we do pairwise programming, we actually learn so much from each other,”

“Every time I watch someone work, I learn something myself as well,”

Anthropic's top engineer Fiona Fung is not liking the effect AI coding agents are having on software developers. She recently said that growing use of these AI coding agents, such as Claude Code, has made coding a more isolated experience, with engineers spending so much time working alongside AI tools that collaboration with colleagues has started to decline.Speaking on the latest episode ofFung said her team observed an unexpected side effect as developers increasingly relied on AI agents for coding tasks. Fung said,Her comments come as AI coding assistants become increasingly common across the software industry. Developers are spending more time managing AI agents, reviewing generated code and coordinating multiple automated tasks, changing how engineering teams work on projects.To counter the isolation that emerged from agent-assisted coding, Anthropic began organising programming lunches, hackathons, and dedicated blocks of sharedThe initiative was designed to bring engineers together in person and allow them to observe how colleagues use AI tools in their daily workflows. According to Fung, developers often use AI systems differently, creating opportunities for teams to learn from one another when working side by side.Fung said. The company found that collaborative coding sessions helped engineers exchange techniques and approaches that may not have been discovered while working alone with AI agents.Claude Code has gained significant adoption among software developers. A survey conducted by Business Insider involving more than two dozen founders and venture capitalists found that Claude Code was widely used within startups, particularly for complex engineering tasks.The rise of AI-assisted software development has also contributed to the growth of “vibecoding,” where users rely on natural-language prompts to build software. The trend has enabled some non-technical founders to create custom applications without hiring traditional engineering teams, contributing to the emergence of solo business operators.Despite these changes, Fung said collaboration remains important for engineering teams. She noted that observing how others interact with AI tools continues to provide valuable learning opportunities.she added.
Article Summary: Anthropic's top engineer Fiona Fung is not liking the effect AI coding agents are having on software developers. She recently said that growing use of these AI coding Agents has made coding a more isolated experience. To counter the isolation, Anthropic began organising programming lunches, hackathons, and dedicated blocks of shared.

Article Title: As Neuralink turns 10, Elon Musk hints sets achieving Brain Telepathy as next goal; says: May be first ...
Article Text:
Elon Musk on Neuralink's 'year-end' plans



"Elon just hinted at Neuralink’s next major leap:

Not just brain-to-computer control

Higher-bandwidth communication between humans, AI, and eventually humans themselves

This is much bigger than typing with your brain

AI is moving at a speed humans simply can’t match through keyboards, phones, voice, or even language itself

We think in rich ideas

Then we compress those ideas into words

Then someone else has to decode those words back into meaning

That is painfully low bandwidth

AI does not have that problem

Machines can process, respond, and improve at insane speed

If humans stay trapped in slow communication loops while AI keeps accelerating, we lose the advantage

Neuralink is the bridge

“In the long term, Neuralink hopes to play a role in AI risk / civilizational risk reduction by improving human-to-AI and human-to-human bandwidth by several orders of magnitude” — Elon Musk

Brain → computer is step one

Brain → AI is the next frontier

Brain → brain is the long-term vision

Human intent moving closer to the speed of thought

This is how humans stay competitive in the age of superintelligence

Not by slowing AI down

By upgrading human bandwidth"
Article Summary: "Elon just hinted at Neuralink’s next major leap: not just brain-to-computer control but higher-bandwidth communication between humans, AI, and eventually humans themselves" "If humans stay trapped in slow communication loops while AI keeps accelerating, we lose the advantage"

Article Title: Amazon Web Services CEO Matt Garman says AI will 'change' jobs and explains why the company is hiring 11,000 interns this year
Article Text:
“If you believe that half of jobs get wiped out, the whole economy collapses on itself. Everything goes away. You’re not going to have AI, and then you have to go back to those other jobs at some point. The math doesn’t work out,”

‘Change, not collapse’: AWS CEO Matt Garman on jobs



“I do think that half of white-collar jobs may change, but wipe out and change are different,”

Why Amazon is still betting on young talent



“When you talk about

entry-level jobs

, number one, they're your cheapest employees,"

“They haven't learned bad habits, you can teach them the culture, they're willing to learn the new tools, they're some of the very best employees you can possibly have.”

“They come in with an energy and excitement, a new view on things. If you just have the exact same people that you've had for the last 15 years, you don't get that energy and excitement and new ideas,"

Amazon Web Services CEO Matt Garman has rejected the fears that artificial intelligence (AI) will eliminate certain roles, arguing that AI will ‘change’ jobs, not wipe them out. He also explained the reason why young talent remains a priority for the company. At Amazon ’s What's Next with AWS event in April, Garman revealed that the company is hiring 11,000 interns this year.Garman said on an episode of the Platformer podcast released on Tuesday (June 23).His take came amid forecasts like the one made by Anthropic CEO Dario Amodei, who had warned that AI could eliminate up to half of all entry-level office jobs.Garman explains the difference between jobs being wiped out and jobs being changed with an example of Microsoft Excel. When spreadsheet software arrived, it largely replaced workers who spent their days doing calculations by hand. Those jobs did not disappear because workers adapted, learned the new tools, and the nature of their roles shifted.he said.What makes Garman’s position particularly notable is that Amazon is backing it with real hiring decisions. The company plans to bring on 11,000 interns and recent graduates this year. Amazon also employs more software developers today than it did two years ago even as AI coding tools have become significantly more capable during that same period.For Garman, young workers are an advantage in an AI-driven world.he said, further noting,Beyond the economics, he pointed to amount of energy that youngsters bring.Garman said.Garman’s optimism comes as Amazon has cut around 30,000 jobs in the last 7 months. CEO Andy Jassy , who is currently in India, has been blunt that AI-driven efficiency gains will eventually reduce parts of the company's corporate headcount.
Article Summary: Amazon Web Services CEO Matt Garman has rejected the fears that artificial intelligence (AI) will eliminate certain roles. Garman argues that AI will ‘change’ jobs, not wipe them out. The company plans to bring on 11,000 interns and recent graduates this year.

Article Title: Philips DST6130/40 steam iron review: The iron that lets you stop worrying about burns
Article Text:
Rating: 4/5

Design

Features and performance

Verdict

Ironing is one of those household tasks that most people want to get through as quickly and painlessly as possible. The last thing anyone needs is the added mental load of remembering which fabric needs which temperature, or the anxiety of leaving an iron face-down on a silk blouse for two seconds too long. Philips, one of the world’s leading ironing brands, has built its latest DST6100 series around solving exactly that problem. The DST6130/40 arrives with a bold promise front and centre: No Burns Guaranteed. That is not a marketing slogan with an asterisk. It is the core design philosophy of this iron, and everything else follows from it. We used the latest DST6130/40 steam iron from Philips to test whether it delivers on its promises and here’s our complete review of the same.Philips has a history of making some of the best steam irons and the newly launched Philips DST6130/40 makes a strong impression. The latest steam iron from Philips comes with a modern fluidic design and feels premium without being ostentatious. The steam iron houses clean lines and a well-balanced body. The aesthetically designed iron looks great when kept on the laundry shelf or the ironing board. Philips DST6130/40 sits comfortably in the hand and the company has also well distributed the weight so that the extended ironing sessions don’t leave you with an aching wrist.The steam iron comes with a 300 ml water tank which helps in long and uninterrupted ironing sessions. The water tank comes with a wide opening which is enough to fill without funnelling water all over the counter, which is a small but genuinely appreciated practical detail. The steam trigger is also positioned well and responds crisply without requiring excessive grip pressure.Philips DST6130/40 comes with the Steam Glide Plus soleplate. The soleplate easily glides across the fabric with very little resistance, including over buttons and seams that tend to catch on cheaper soleplates. The surface of the soleplate is scratch-resistant and durable and even after repeated use across different fabric types it shows no signs of wear or discolouration. Philips has clearly invested in the soleplate quality here, and it shows in everyday use.The headline feature of the DST6130/40 is OptimalTEMP Technology, and it is the reason this iron occupies a distinct position in the market rather than being just another competent steam iron. OptimalTEMP means there is no temperature dial on this iron. None. You fill the tank, switch it on, wait for it to heat up, and start ironing. The technology maintains the iron at a temperature that is safe for every ironable fabric, from the most delicate silk and synthetics through to heavy cotton, linen, and denim. You can move from a polyester blouse to a linen shirt to a cotton bedsheet without pausing, sorting, or adjusting anything. The iron handles the transition itself.The practical consequence of this is the No Burns Guaranteed claim. Because the iron never exceeds the temperature threshold beyond which delicate fabrics burn, the risk of scorching is eliminated on any ironable garment. You can set the iron face-down on a silk garment and walk away to answer the door, and the fabric will be fine. For anyone who has ever ruined a favourite shirt with an iron that was set a notch too high, this technology is genuinely transformative.The 2800W power rating on the DST6130/40 drives continuous steam output of up to 50g per minute, with a steam boost of 220g for tackling particularly stubborn creases in heavy fabrics. Both figures are strong for this category. The Auto Shut-Off function adds another layer of safety, switching the iron off automatically when it is left unattended, whether lying flat or standing upright. Drip Stop Technology prevents water from leaking onto fabric at lower temperatures, which eliminates the water spotting that plagues cheaper irons.In daily use across a range of fabrics, the DST6130/40 performs exactly as Philips promises. Cotton shirts come out crisp and wrinkle-free with minimal passes. Linen, which can be notoriously stubborn, responds well to the steam boost, with heavy creases releasing cleanly without needing to go over the same area repeatedly. On delicate fabrics including polyester and silk blends, the iron glides without incident, and the OptimalTEMP protection removes the usual anxiety entirely.The heat-up time is fast, reaching working temperature in under a minute from cold. The continuous steam output is consistent rather than patchy, which makes a meaningful difference when working through a pile of clothes — you are not stopping to wait for steam to build back up between garments. The steam boost button delivers a strong, focused burst that handles denim waistbands and collar fabric that continuous steam alone would struggle with.The soleplate's glide performance is one of the standout real-world qualities of this iron. It moves smoothly over all fabric types without snagging, and the steam holes are distributed well enough that steam reaches the fabric evenly rather than concentrating in patches. Over heavy cotton bedsheets, where a poor glide surface becomes tiring quickly, the DST6130/40 is noticeably easier to manage than mid-range alternatives.The Auto Shut-Off works reliably and kicks in within a short window of inactivity, which provides genuine peace of mind without being so aggressive that it switches off while you are simply repositioning a garment.Priced at Rs 11,959, the Philips DST6130/40 is a well-built, thoughtfully designed steam iron that delivers on its central promise. OptimalTEMP Technology works exactly as described, and the freedom to iron any garment without adjusting settings or sorting fabrics by type is a quality-of-life upgrade that is difficult to go back to once you have experienced it. The No Burns Guarantee is not a gimmick, it is the practical outcome of a genuine engineering choice, and it makes this iron meaningfully safer and more convenient than temperature-dial alternatives at similar or higher price points. The 2800W power, 50g continuous steam, and 220g boost are all strong specifications that translate into real-world performance. The Steam Glide Plus soleplate is excellent. The Auto Shut-Off and Drip Stop features round off a safety package that is comprehensive for a home iron.
Article Summary: Philips DST6130/40 is the latest steam iron from one of the world's leading ironing brands. The steam iron houses clean lines and a well-balanced body. OptimalTEMP technology maintains the iron at a temperature that is safe for every ironable fabric.
